disease.name: Fibrocystic Breast Disease
disease.note: Benign breast condition characterized by cyclic breast pain, nodularity, and cyst formation driven by hormonal fluctuations. Most common in women 30-50 years. In Kenya, this is a frequent cause of breast clinic visits due to health-seeking anxiety about breast cancer. Symptoms typically resolve after menopause. The key is to distinguish from malignancy — no dominant mass, no skin changes, no lymphadenopathy.
presentingComplaints: Breast pain that comes and goes with periods
Lumpy feeling in the breasts, especially before menses
Breast swelling and fullness before periods
Both breasts feel tender or painful

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Atypical hyperplasia/complex cyst with atypia -> SEVERE (increased cancer risk); simple fibrocystic changes -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When do you experience breast pain in relation to your period?
Is the pain worse before your period and better after?
Do you feel lumps that come and go with your cycle?
Is it one breast or both?
How old are you?
Are your periods regular?
Do you have a family history of breast cancer?
Have you noticed a specific lump that does not change with your cycle?
Do you have any nipple discharge?
Have you had children — did you breastfeed?
Are you on any hormonal medication or contraception?
Do you drink coffee, tea, or soda?
Does the pain wake you up at night?
Is there any redness, warmth, or skin changes?
Have you noticed any lump in your armpit?
Have you had mammogram or ultrasound before?
Do you have any other symptoms like weight loss or fatigue?
Are you worried this might be cancer?
Have you ever been told you have 'lumpy breasts' before?
Does anyone else in your family have similar breast symptoms?
What makes the pain better or worse?
Have you tried any treatments for the pain — painkillers, oils, or herbs?
Have you seen a traditional healer for this?
How many children have you had and at what age did you have your first child?
Did you breastfeed your children and for how long?
Are you still having periods or have you reached menopause?
Do you take hormone replacement therapy?
Has your breast pain ever caused you to miss work?
Do you have any discharge from your nipple(s)?
Do you smoke or drink alcohol?
Have you had any breast surgery before?
Are you under a lot of stress lately?
Do you feel generally well otherwise — any weight loss, fatigue, or bone pain?

examination_findings.finding: Nodular breast tissue — diffuse or bilateral
Ropey or cord-like thickening of breast tissue
Palpable cysts — smooth, tense, variable size
Tenderness on palpation (especially pre-menstrual)
Upper outer quadrant predominance of nodularity
Bilateral involvement (symmetrical)
No dominant or discrete mass
No skin tethering or dimpling
No axillary lymphadenopathy
No nipple retraction
Nodularity changes on re-examination at different phase of cycle
Breast swelling with generalized fullness
Ill-defined thickened areas without discrete borders
Cysts may transilluminate (fluid-filled)
Nipple may be sensitive to touch but not retracted
Breast tissue feels 'pebbly' or 'cobblestone'-like
No warmth or erythema of overlying skin
Pain may be reproduced by gentle pressure with two fingers
Parenchymal texture markedly different from surrounding normal breast
Cysts may feel tense and tender pre-menstrually, softer post-menstrually

bedside_scores.name: Mastalgia Severity Score (Cardiff Breast Pain Score)

components.name: Pain frequency (0: none, 1: cyclic <7days, 2: cyclic >7days, 3: non-cyclic)
Pain severity (0: none, 1: mild, 2: moderate, 3: severe)
Impact on daily activities (0: none, 1: some, 2: moderate, 3: severe)

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0-3: mild — reassurance only; 4-6: moderate — consider NSAIDs, supportive bra, evening primrose oil; 7-10: severe — consider hormonal therapy (Danazol, Tamoxifen, or OCP), refer to breast clinic

subtypes.name: Non-Proliferative Fibrocystic Change
subtypes.management: Reassurance. Well-fitted supportive bra. Analgesia (NSAIDs). Evening primrose oil. Avoid caffeine. Oral contraceptives if severe. Symptomatic cysts can be aspirated.

subtypes.name: Proliferative without Atypia
subtypes.management: Reassurance. Regular follow-up. Clinical breast exam every 6-12 months. Annual imaging. Consider risk-reducing strategies if strong family history.

subtypes.name: Atypical Ductal/Lobular Hyperplasia (ADH/ALH)
subtypes.management: Close surveillance: annual mammogram + MRI. Clinical exam every 6 months. Risk-reducing medications (Tamoxifen 20mg daily for 5 years). Consider genetic counseling if family history. Lifestyle modification.

bedside.name: Clinical breast exam at different phases of menstrual cycle
Cyst aspiration
bedside.indication: For dominant, painful or enlarging cysts — therapeutic and diagnostic

laboratory.name: None routinely — fibrocystic changes are a clinical and imaging diagnosis
laboratory.indication: Not indicated unless atypia or malignancy suspected

imaging.name: Breast Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Characterize cysts (simple, complex, complicated), assess nodularity

imaging.name: Mammogram
imaging.indication: Baseline for age >35, excludes occult malignancy in dense tissue

imaging.name: Fine needle aspiration of dominant cyst
imaging.indication: Therapeutic for symptomatic cysts, send fluid for cytology if blood-stained or complex

imaging.name: Core biopsy if imaging suspicious
imaging.indication: Rule out atypia or malignancy for complex cysts or suspicious imaging features

management.protocolReference: ACOG Practice Bulletin — Benign Breast Disease / ACR Guidelines

conservative: Reassurance is the mainstay — explanation of benign cyclic condition
Well-fitted supportive bra (sports bra during painful periods)
Simple analgesia: NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400mg TDS) during pain episodes
Evening primrose oil 1-2g daily or Vitamin E 400IU daily for mastalgia
Reduce or avoid caffeine (coffee, tea, soda, chocolate)
Heat or cold packs for symptomatic relief
Reduce dietary fat intake if high

surgical: Cyst aspiration for symptomatic or dominant cysts
Excision biopsy of complex cysts or persistent dominant masses
Excision of atypical hyperplasia lesions for definitive diagnosis
No prophylactic mastectomy for fibrocystic disease without atypia
nonOperative: For severe cyclical mastalgia not responding to conservative measures:
Oral contraceptive pill (cycle regulation reduces hormonal peaks)
Danazol 100-200mg daily (androgenic side effects limit use)
Tamoxifen 10-20mg daily for 3-6 months (effective but side effects)
Bromocriptine — rarely used due to side effects
All hormonal therapy requires careful discussion of risks and benefits

immediate: Pressure dressing for 24h after cyst aspiration or biopsy

pathways.criteria: simple cysts, fibrosis, no epithelial hyperplasia, no atypia, concordant imaging and clinical findings
pathways.treatment: Reassurance. Symptom management: supportive bra, NSAIDs, evening primrose oil, reduced caffeine. Simple cyst aspiration if painful. Discharge from breast clinic.

pathways.criteria: atypical ductal or lobular hyperplasia on biopsy
pathways.treatment: Close surveillance (annual MRI + mammogram). 6-month clinical exam. Risk-reduction counseling. Discuss Tamoxifen for risk reduction. Genetic counseling referral. Lifestyle modification.

operative_templates.indication: Symptomatic dominant breast cyst — painful, tense, or causing anxiety
operative_templates.approach: US-guided or blind aspiration of palpable cyst
operative_templates.position: Supine with ipsilateral arm behind head
operative_templates.incision: No incision — 21G or 23G needle puncture through cleansed skin
steps: Clean skin over cyst with antiseptic
Insert needle attached to 10ml syringe into cyst cavity
Aspirate fluid completely — cyst should fully collapse
Observe fluid color: straw-yellow is benign, blood-stained requires cytology
If residual mass felt after aspiration: mark for biopsy
Apply pressure to site for 2 minutes
keyLandmarks: Palpable cyst — center of maximum fluctuance
Ultrasound guidance if cyst is deep or small

watchFor: return of palpable lump at same site

dischargeCriteria: Patient reassured and understands benign nature
For atypical hyperplasia: close follow-up plan in place

patient_education.explanation: Fibrocystic breast disease is a benign (non-cancerous) condition where your breast tissue feels lumpy, thick, and sometimes painful. This is very common in women aged 30-50 and is related to the normal hormonal changes of your menstrual cycle. It is NOT cancer and does not become cancer in the vast majority of cases. In a small number of women with certain cell changes on biopsy, there is a slightly increased risk that requires closer follow-up.
warningSigns: A new lump that stays in one place and does not change with your cycle
A lump that continues to grow
Skin changes like dimpling or orange peel
A new lump in your armpit
activity: No restrictions — normal activity is encouraged
Wear a well-fitting supportive bra, especially during exercise
Sports bras may help during painful periods
diet: Reducing or avoiding caffeine (coffee, tea, soda, chocolate) may help some women
Low-fat diet may help reduce symptoms
Evening primrose oil or Vitamin E supplements may help breast pain
medication: If prescribed hormonal therapy (Danazol, Tamoxifen, OCP): take as directed
No routine medication needed for most women
followUp: If simple fibrocystic changes: no special follow-up needed — return only if new symptoms
If biopsy showed atypical hyperplasia: follow-up with breast surgeon every 6 months with annual MRI and mammogram

before_discharge: Advice on caffeine reduction, supportive bra, NSAIDs

after_discharge.action: Review any biopsy results if taken
Clinical breast exam if proliferative disease or atypia
Annual mammogram + clinical exam for atypical hyperplasia
Recheck symptomatic cysts if aspirated — US if recurrence

when_to_return: Develop a new persistent breast lump
Breast pain that is severe and not relieved by NSAIDs
Skin changes or nipple discharge appears
Cyst returns after aspiration and is painful

pathophysiologyDetail: Fibrocystic changes represent an exaggerated physiologic response of breast tissue to cyclic hormonal fluctuations — estrogen stimulates ductal proliferation and stromal edema, while progesterone normally counteracts this
Estrogen dominance (relative or absolute) is the key driver: estradiol increases vascular permeability and stromal hydration, causing breast swelling, nodularity, and pain in the luteal phase
Cyst formation occurs when dilated ducts and acini become obstructed by epithelial hyperplasia and dense stromal fibrosis, causing fluid accumulation under pressure

Apocrine metaplasia is a hallmark histological finding — the epithelial cells lining cysts acquire features of apocrine sweat glands (abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, apical snouts), reflecting hormone receptor activation
Sclerosing adenosis involves proliferation of distorted acini and myoepithelial cells within dense fibrous stroma — it can mimic carcinoma clinically and mammographically

Ductal epithelial hyperplasia (usual type) is a proliferative response involving expansion of epithelial cells into ductal lumens — it is NOT atypical and carries only mildly increased cancer risk (RR 1.5-2.0)
Atypical ductal hyperplasia (ADH) shares some cytologic features with low-grade DCIS but is quantitatively less extensive — it carries a 4-5x increased risk of subsequent breast cancer
The terminal ductal lobular unit (TDLU) is the epicenter of fibrocystic changes — involution of TDLUs with age leads to microcyst formation, fibrosis, and loss of lobular architecture
Prolactin may play a role: elevated prolactin levels (stress, certain medications) can exacerbate breast pain and nodularity by stimulating epithelial proliferation
Caffeine and methylxanthines are thought to exacerbate symptoms through inhibition of phosphodiesterase, increasing intracellular cAMP in breast tissue, though evidence is mixed
In Kenyan women, fibrocystic changes are frequently misattributed to malignancy due to limited breast health literacy and anxiety about breast cancer — education is as important as treatment
Androgen receptor expression in breast epithelium may modulate estrogenic effects — low AR expression is associated with more severe symptoms and higher breast cancer risk in proliferative disease

landmarkTrials.trial: Dupont WD, Page DL. Risk factors for breast cancer in women with proliferative breast disease
landmarkTrials.population: 10,366 breast biopsy specimens from women at Vanderbilt University followed for 17 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Non-proliferative disease: no increased risk (RR 1.0). Proliferative without atypia: RR 1.9. Atypical hyperplasia: RR 4.4-5.3. Family history + atypia: RR 11
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark study that established the risk stratification system for benign breast disease, still in use today
landmarkTrials.reference: Dupont WD, Page DL. N Engl J Med. 1985;312(3):146-151

landmarkTrials.trial: NSABP P-1 (BCPT) Breast Cancer Prevention Trial: Tamoxifen for risk reduction
landmarkTrials.population: 13,388 high-risk women including those with atypical hyperplasia, followed for 5 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Tamoxifen reduced breast cancer risk by 86% in women with atypical hyperplasia (from 10.1% to 1.4% at 5 years)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established Tamoxifen as a risk-reducing agent for women with atypical hyperplasia, the highest-risk benign breast disease group
landmarkTrials.reference: Fisher B, et al. N Engl J Med. 1998;339(16):1115-1125

landmarkTrials.trial: Preece PE, et al. Evening primrose oil for mastalgia — a randomized controlled trial
landmarkTrials.population: 202 women with severe cyclic mastalgia randomized to evening primrose oil vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Evening primrose oil (gamolenic acid) reduced breast pain by 45% vs 20% placebo at 6 months
landmarkTrials.impact: Established evening primrose oil as a standard first-line treatment for cyclic mastalgia
landmarkTrials.reference: Preece PE, et al. Br J Surg. 1982;69(9):531-534

landmarkTrials.trial: Gateley CA, et al. Danazol for cyclical mastalgia — dose-response study
landmarkTrials.population: 100 women with severe cyclic mastalgia treated with different doses of Danazol
landmarkTrials.finding: Danazol 200mg daily achieved 70% pain reduction but with significant side effects (androgenic, weight gain). 100mg was less effective but better tolerated
landmarkTrials.impact: Defined the risk-benefit of Danazol for severe mastalgia — effective but limited by side effects
landmarkTrials.reference: Gateley CA, et al. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 1990;30(3):437-443

landmarkTrials.trial: O'Brien PMS, Abukhalil I. Randomized controlled trial of bromocriptine for cyclical mastalgia
landmarkTrials.population: 78 women with severe cyclic mastalgia randomized to bromocriptine vs placebo for 6 months
landmarkTrials.finding: Bromocriptine reduced pain scores by 40% vs 15% placebo but with 35% dropout due to nausea and dizziness
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed prolactin suppression can help mastalgia but side effects limit clinical utility
landmarkTrials.reference: O'Brien PMS, Abukhalil I. Br J Obstet Gynaecol. 1986;93(6):618-623

landmarkTrials.trial: Hartmann LC, et al. Benign breast disease and the risk of breast cancer
landmarkTrials.population: 9,087 women with benign breast disease from the Mayo Clinic, median follow-up 15 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Overall breast cancer risk was 1.5x population. Risk stratified by histology: non-proliferative RR 1.3, proliferative without atypia RR 1.9, atypical hyperplasia RR 4.1
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated the Dupont-Page classification in a modern cohort with long follow-up, confirming risk stratification
landmarkTrials.reference: Hartmann LC, et al. N Engl J Med. 2005;353(3):229-237

evidenceTable.recommendation: Non-proliferative fibrocystic changes require no follow-up beyond routine breast cancer screening
evidenceTable.reference: Dupont WD, N Engl J Med 1985; Hartmann LC, N Engl J Med 2005

evidenceTable.recommendation: Proliferative fibrocystic disease without atypia warrants clinical breast exam every 6-12 months and annual mammography
evidenceTable.reference: NCCN Breast Cancer Risk Reduction Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Atypical ductal/lobular hyperplasia warrants annual mammogram + MRI alternating, clinical exam every 6 months, and risk-reduction counseling
evidenceTable.reference: NCCN Guidelines; Hartmann LC, N Engl J Med 2005

evidenceTable.recommendation: Tamoxifen 20mg daily for 5 years reduces breast cancer risk by 86% in women with atypical hyperplasia
evidenceTable.reference: NSABP P-1, Fisher B, N Engl J Med 1998

evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line management of cyclic mastalgia: reassurance, well-fitted supportive bra, simple analgesia (NSAIDs)
Evening primrose oil 1-2g daily is effective as second-line therapy for cyclic mastalgia
Danazol 100-200mg daily is effective for severe cyclic mastalgia but use limited by androgenic side effects
evidenceTable.reference: Gateley CA, Br J Clin Pharmacol 1990

evidenceTable.recommendation: Simple cyst aspiration is therapeutic and diagnostic; send fluid for cytology ONLY if blood-stained or residual mass
Caffeine reduction may help some women with cyclic mastalgia despite mixed trial evidence
evidenceTable.reference: Levinson W, et al. J Gen Intern Med. 1986;1(4):225-228

evidenceTable.recommendation: Oral contraceptives improve cyclic mastalgia by stabilizing hormonal fluctuations
evidenceTable.reference: Morrow M. The evaluation of common breast problems. Am Fam Physician 2000

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Woman presenting with breast pain and/or lumpiness → Clinical breast exam — document: bilateral vs unilateral, presence/absence of dominant mass, nodularity pattern, cyst areas, axillary nodes, skin/nipple changes → Cyclic pattern? Pain worse pre-menstrually, better post-menstrually, nodules that come and go → Likely fibrocystic changes. Non-cyclic pain or focal finding → Consider alternative diagnosis. → Age <35 and classic cyclic pattern → Reassurance + symptomatic management. If atypical features or patient anxious → Breast Ultrasound. → Age ≥35 → Bilateral mammogram + targeted US. Assess BI-RADS. → Imaging shows simple cysts (BI-RADS 2) → Reassurance. Aspirate if painful. → Imaging shows complex cyst or suspicious features (BI-RADS 3-4) → Core needle biopsy. → Biopsy result: Non-proliferative → Discharge. Proliferative without atypia → Clinical exam + annual mammogram. Atypical hyperplasia → Close surveillance (MRI + mammogram q12mo alternating), risk-reduction counseling, Tamoxifen discussion, genetic counseling if family history. → All patients: symptom management algorithm — Step 1: Supportive bra, NSAIDs, cold packs, caffeine reduction, EPO/vitamin E. Step 2: OCP (if not contraindicated). Step 3: Danazol 100-200mg daily or Tamoxifen 10mg for 3-6 months for severe cases. → Follow-up: annual clinical breast exam; imaging per risk category.

managementPearls.context: Reassurance is the most powerful intervention
managementPearls.pearl: Up to 60% of women with fibrocystic changes are primarily anxious about cancer, not in pain. A thorough clinical exam + explanation that 'this is a normal variant of breast tissue' resolves symptoms in most patients. Never underestimate the therapeutic value of a careful breast exam.
managementPearls.evidence: Clinical experience; Morrow M. Am Fam Physician 2000

managementPearls.pearl: Breast pain is almost NEVER a symptom of breast cancer. Less than 1% of breast cancers present with pain alone. Reassure patients: 'pain is good news — cancers rarely hurt.' This simple statement transforms the consultation for many anxious women.
managementPearls.evidence: Morrow M. The evaluation of common breast problems. Am Fam Physician 2000

managementPearls.pearl: Aspirate only if the cyst is painful, tense, or causing anxiety. If aspirated: the fluid MUST be straw-colored (benign) and the lump MUST completely disappear. If fluid is bloody OR a residual mass remains: send for cytology and arrange biopsy. Never aspirate a complex cyst — it can obscure the diagnosis.
managementPearls.evidence: ACR BI-RADS Atlas; NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary

managementPearls.context: ECRBSP risk model for Kenyan women
managementPearls.pearl: For Kenyan women with atypical hyperplasia, use the IBIS/Tyrer-Cuzick risk model to calculate 10-year and lifetime breast cancer risk. A 10-year risk >5% or lifetime risk >20% qualifies for risk-reducing strategies (Tamoxifen, enhanced surveillance, genetic testing).
managementPearls.evidence: Tyrer J, et al. A breast cancer prediction model. Stat Med 2004

managementPearls.context: When to refer for genetic testing
managementPearls.pearl: Refer for genetic counseling if: atypical hyperplasia WITH family history of breast/ovarian cancer, age at diagnosis <50, bilateral or multifocal ADH, multiple family members with BRCA-related cancers, or patient of Ashkenazi Jewish descent. In Kenya, limited access to genetic testing means clinical risk models are even more important.

Danazol is effective but poorly tolerated. Start at 100mg daily (half the typical starting dose) and titrate up. Warn about: weight gain (most common reason for discontinuation), deepening of voice (irreversible), acne, hirsutism, and hot flashes. Use only for severe mastalgia that fails all other measures. Max 3-6 month courses.
managementPearls.evidence: Gateley CA. Br J Clin Pharmacol 1990

managementPearls.pearl: Tamoxifen 10mg daily for 3-6 months is extremely effective for severe cyclic mastalgia (80% response) and can be used when Danazol fails. Off-label use in Kenya requires thorough discussion of side effects: hot flashes (50%), vaginal discharge (20%), VTE risk (1%), endometrial cancer (rare but serious with prolonged use).
managementPearls.evidence: Fentiman IS, et al. Tamoxifen and mastalgia. Br J Surg 1988

managementPearls.pearl: Fibrocystic changes and cyclic mastalgia resolve naturally at menopause. For women nearing menopause (>45-48), conservative management and reassurance are even more appropriate. Hormone replacement therapy can 're-activate' fibrocystic changes — counsel accordingly.
managementPearls.evidence: Santen RJ, et al. Endocrine aspects of benign breast disease. Endocr Relat Cancer 2005

operativeNuances.pitfall: Using too small a needle (25G or smaller) — thick cyst fluid cannot be aspirated, the procedure fails, and the patient experiences unnecessary pain
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a minimum of 21G needle for cyst aspiration. For large cysts or thick fluid, an 18G needle attached to a 20ml syringe creates sufficient negative pressure. If the fluid is greenish-brown (old cyst content), it may be toothpaste-thick — use a larger bore needle or ask ultrasound to assess septations.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Aspirating a complex cyst with solid components, potentially missing an intracystic papilloma or carcinoma
operativeNuances.expertTip: Only aspirate simple cysts (anechoic, thin-walled, posterior enhancement). Never aspirate a complex cyst with septations, mural nodule, or thick wall. These require core biopsy. If you inadvertently puncture a complex cyst, document carefully and proceed to biopsy.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete excision of ADH found on core biopsy — up to 20% of cases are upgraded to DCIS or invasive cancer at excision
operativeNuances.expertTip: When excising for ADH found on core biopsy: place a localization wire, excise with 1cm margin around the clip, orient the specimen, and request specimen radiography to confirm the clip is within the specimen. Despite this, 10-20% will be upgraded — counsel the patient preoperatively.

operativeNuances.procedure: Multiple Cyst Aspirations During One Visit
operativeNuances.pitfall: Bilateral multiple cyst aspirations causing significant discomfort and high risk of hematoma
operativeNuances.expertTip: Prioritize: aspirate only the most symptomatic cyst(s). Bilateral extensive aspiration in one sitting is painful and risks hematoma. If multiple cysts need aspiration, do the largest/most painful one and schedule a second session. Apply firm pressure for 3-5 minutes after each aspiration.

operativeNuances.procedure: Fine Needle Aspiration of Palpable Cyst Without US
operativeNuances.pitfall: Blind aspiration of a suspected cyst that is actually a solid mass, yielding inadequate or non-diagnostic material
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always confirm the lesion is a cyst before needling. If ultrasound is not available, check for transillumination (cysts transilluminate, solid masses do not). If there is any doubt, the 'cyst' should be imaged first. A 'failed aspiration' (no fluid) should be treated as a solid mass needing core biopsy.

prognosis.mortality: No mortality attributable to fibrocystic changes themselves. Atypical hyperplasia carries 4-5x increased breast cancer risk, corresponding to a 10-year risk of 4-8% and lifetime risk of 20-30%. Non-proliferative disease has no increased mortality. Proliferative without atypia carries a small (1.5-2x) risk increase.
prognosis.morbidity: Quality of life can be significantly impaired by cyclic mastalgia in 10-20% of affected women — 5% report severe pain affecting sleep, work, and sexual function. Breast nodularity causes ongoing cancer anxiety. Cyst recurrence is common (30% at 1 year). Surgical scars from biopsies cause cosmetic concern in some.
prognosis.recurrence: Cysts recur in 30% of women within 1 year of aspiration. Fibrocystic nodularity persists throughout reproductive years and regresses at menopause. Atypical hyperplasia does not 'recur' but is a marker for elevated breast cancer risk that persists for 15-20 years after diagnosis.
prognosis.survival: Overall survival is normal for non-proliferative and proliferative without atypia. For atypical hyperplasia: 5-year breast cancer-free survival is 95-97%, 10-year is 90-95%, and 20-year is 75-85% (depending on family history and risk-reducing interventions). Tamoxifen reduces the risk of developing breast cancer by 50% in ADH.
prognosis.reference: Dupont WD, Page DL. N Engl J Med 1985. Hartmann LC, et al. N Engl J Med 2005. Fisher B, NSABP P-1. N Engl J Med 1998.
